title: North & West Africa
gist: An Atlantic Council North & West Africa landing page whose teasers surface investable signals: Algeria off the FATF grey list, Guinea's minerals as a US-China contest, Burkina Faso as the Sahel's new violence epicenter, and Russia recruiting African soldiers for Ukraine.

insights.claim: The Financial Action Task Force recently removed Algeria from its grey list, a marker of financial-reform progress that Washington could leverage to strengthen ties.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: FATF grey-list removal lowers compliance friction and can improve Algeria's access to international finance and investment.
insights.locator: Algeria's financial reforms are showing progress

insights.claim: Egypt's land development and monetization efforts will have measurable economic benefits but are unlikely by themselves to put the country's economy on a solid footing.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Land monetization is insufficient as a standalone fix, keeping Egypt's macro fragility a watch item for EM investors.

insights.claim: Guinea's democratic transition and mineral riches place it at the center of US-China competition, giving the United States a rare chance to align investment, security, and governance to secure strategic influence in West Africa.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Guinea's critical minerals make it a contested arena; watch for US investment and security moves to counter Chinese positioning.
insights.locator: In Guinea, the US has a rare opportunity

insights.claim: Burkina Faso, not headline-grabbing Mali, has become the Sahel's true epicenter of extremist violence, as President Ibrahim Traoré tightens his grip on power.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Burkina Faso's escalating insecurity and authoritarian consolidation raise regional instability risk across West Africa.
insights.locator: The Sahel's most acute crisis is unfolding

insights.claim: Mali's security situation became critical after a large-scale, coordinated series of attacks across multiple cities on April 25, 2026.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Coordinated multi-city attacks signal deteriorating state control in Mali, a risk for regional stability and resource projects.
insights.locator: What can save Mali from collapse?

insights.claim: As Russian battlefield losses mount, Moscow is increasingly turning to African recruits to sustain its war in Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Atlantic Council
insights.so_what: Reliance on foreign African recruits signals Russian manpower strain, a marker of the invasion's cost and Russia's Africa leverage.
